document.docAuthor: Xu, Zaifu, Olmi, Massimo & He, Junhua
document.masterDocTitle: Dryinidae of the Oriental region (Hymenoptera: Chrysidoidea)

mods:titleInfo.mods:title: Dryinidae of the Oriental region (Hymenoptera: Chrysidoidea)

paragraph.treatmentCitationGroup:
Monogonatopus Richards 1939: 200
(synonymized by Olmi & Currado 1979b ); type species:
Monogonatopus oratorius ( Westwood 1833 )
, by monotypy.

Congodryinus Benoit 1950: 223
(synonymized by Olmi 1984 ); type species:
Congodryinus katangae Benoit 1950
, by original designation.

subSubSection.paragraph:
Type species.
Haplogonatopus apicalis R. Perkins, 1905
, by original designation.

Diagnosis. Female : apterous ( Plates 103A , 105A ); palpal formula 2/1; antenna with rhinaria; pronotum not crossed by strong transverse furrow or weakly impressed ( Plate 105A ); enlarged claw with one large subapical tooth and one row of lamellae ( Plates 103B , 104D ); tibial spurs 1/0/1. Male : fully winged ( Plates 104A , 105B ); occipital carina usually absent, occasionally incomplete, then only present behind and shortly on sides of posterior ocelli; occiput concave; temple present; palpal formula 2/1; tibial spurs 1/1/2.
World species. Seven species are known, two in the Oriental region .

Remarks. Larvae of
Haplogonatopus
were described by Hernandez & Bellotti (1984) .

The females of
H. apicalis
and
H. oratorius
are not always easily separable based of the present knowledge, mainly when the metasoma is brown.

paragraph.keyLead:
- Metasoma testaceous, or reddish, occasionally partly brown; occasionally metasoma totally brown...
H. apicalis R. Perkins

1. Dorsal process of paramere broader ( Plate 104B )...........................................
H. apicalis R. Perkins

- Dorsal process of paramere slender ( Plate 104C ).........................................
H. oratorius (Westwood)

subSubSection.paragraph:
Remarks. The females of
Haplogonatopus apicalis
and
oratorius
are separable with a lot of difficulties on the basis of morphologic characters; the males can be recognized more easily because of the different dorsal processes of the parameres. Apparently in Japan there is a biological difference: according to Esaki & Hashimoto (1935) , Kitamura (1987a) and Mita et al . (2012), in Japan
H. apicalis
(=
H. japonicus Esaki & Hashimoto, 1931
) parasitizes mainly
Sogatella furcifera (Horváth)
, whereas
H. oratorius
(=
H. atratus Esaki & Hashimoto, 1931
) attacks mainly
Laodelphax striatellus (Fallén)
. According to Mita et al . (2012),
S. furcifera
does not overwinters in Japan ; every spring it migrates from Korea and China to Japan , where it is parasitized mainly by
H. apicalis
. According to Mita et al . (2012), the 99.8% of the planthoppers parasitized by
H. apicalis
are belonging to
S. furcifera
; very rarely
H. apicalis
can parasitize other planthoppers. On the contrary, according to Mita et al . (2012),
H. oratorius
parasitizes many species of Delphacidae , but rarely
S. furcifera
. Maybe
H. apicalis
and
H. oratorius
are sister species well separated from a biological point of view.
